title: A new method to analyse bodywide drug effects
html_body:
Biotechnology, drug discovery and pharma |
View this email in your browser Wednesday 1 April 2026

Hello Nature readers,
This week, we hear that a drug for osteoarthritis treats more than just symptoms, watch in vivo CAR-T cells cut enrollment-to-treatment time from weeks to hours and discuss the UNICORN framework for rare paediatric diseases.
A new analysis approach captures RNAs to identify cell types — T cells (dark blue), B cells (light blue) and cells that make red blood cells (red) — and could demonstrate the impact of drugs across an entire animal. (Margarette Clevenger)

New way to analyse bodywide drug effects

Researchers have developed a method that can measure gene expression across an entire animal at once, which could offer a way to measure the impact of drugs across different organs. Typically, scientists analyse the impact of drugs on organs one by one. The new approach uses a specialized machine to cut thin slices of a frozen body and uses a molecular technique to look at gene activity across thousands of locations in each slice. “This is a very beautiful demonstration of a promise that has been floating in the field for a while,” says microbiologist Jeffrey Moffitt. The technique could underestimate drug-related gene expression changes, but is an exciting step, adds Moffitt.

Science | 7 min read
 Reference: Cell paper

A potential first for osteoarthritis treatment

A new submission to the US Food and Drug Administration (FDA) aims to be the first approved drug for osteoarthritis that treats more than its symptoms. Current drugs for osteoarthritis — an inflammatory disease of the joints — only address symptoms. The new drug, lorecivivint, improved pain, function and joint structure compared to a placebo in a trial in knee osteoarthritis, the first drug to do so in all of those three aspects. But modest trial outcomes and some subpar phase III trials results mean some rheumatologists are unsure it will succeed. The FDA has not yet confirmed that it has accepted the drug, but other disease-modifying treatments are in the works.

Nature Reviews Drug Discovery | 12 min read

Infographic of the week
Researchers have used targeted lipid nanoparticles (LNPs) to deliver therapeutic mRNA to the lining of mouse uteri, restoring the ability for embryos to implant in an injured section (right picture) compared to a control group (left picture). (Nature Biomedical Engineering | 4 min read)
Reference: Nature Nanotechnology paper (Images taken by Saed Abbasi)

Features and opinion

Five pillars for bettering precision medicine

Scientists have put forward five principles — understanding disease variation, generalizability and benchmarking, designing precision clinical trials and considering societal risks and benefits — to help real-life clinical impact keep pace with research when it comes to precision medicine. Outside of oncology, there has been limited success in translating precision medicine research into useful clinical outcomes. But there are examples, such as personalized hypertension management, that can be used as a rubric for real-life rollout. Following these guidelines could help minimize health inequities, reduce costs and improve health and well-being, write the authors.

Nature Medicine | 52 min read

A new era for speedy immunotherapy

A new class of cancer-fighting CAR-T cells is emerging that could be scalable in a way that current CAR-T cells that are made outside of the body aren’t, argue immunologists Beatrice Greco, Matteo Doglio and Monica Casucci. Currently, CAR-T-cell therapy involves T cell removal, genetic engineering and infusion of the engineered cells back into the person undergoing therapy: a slow and arduous process. In vivo CAR-T allows the CAR-T cells to be produced inside the person’s own body. Researchers have reported early data using a lentivirus vector to generate multiple myeloma-fighting CAR-T cells within the body. This cut enrollment-to-treatment time to eight hours, compared to several weeks for ex vivo CAR T.

Nature Medicine | 9 min read
Read more:
News about a two-vector system that stops in vivo cancer-fighting CAR T cells accidentally targeting other cells (Nature | 5 min read)
Medicinal chemist Derek Lowe’s thoughts on the latest CAR-T work (Science | 5 min read)
 Reference: Nature Medicine paper

A blueprint for rare gene therapy approvals

Researchers are trying to close the gap between scientific potential and the real-world impacts of gene therapy for rare paediatric diseases. The Unifying Cell Therapy Outcome Prediction and Regulatory Navigation (UNICORN) framework addresses common approval challenges for therapies for rare pediatric diseases, such as small trial populations and a lack of confidence from regulators. The researchers have developed a platform that looks at the characteristics of patients and drugs in these small clinical trials and uses AI to find other clinical data with similar patients and drugs that could be used to help regulators make approval decisions. The goal is to continuously build the evidence base for rare gene therapies to shorten the distance from vial to clinical benefit, write the authors.

Nature Medicine | 4 min read

Interview of the month
(Pfizer)

Pfizer rejigs its priorities

Chris Boshoff, the new head of research and development at Pfizer, has spent his first year on the job reorganizing the company’s focus onto four areas: oncology, internal medicine, inflammation and immunology and vaccines. (Nature Reviews Drug Discovery | 12 min read)

Quote of the week

“When medicine becomes primarily a vehicle for national prestige, everyone loses.”

Health policy scholar Y. Tony Yang argues that pharmaceutical innovation shouldn’t be a zero-sum national contest. (Science | 2 min read)

I was driven into the sciences as a teenager by a hunger for clarity. While studying science can often bring more questions than it does answers, I — like Derek Lowe in this opinion piece — believe striving for truth is the best option.

Please send your favourite philosophical quote — and your feedback — to trbriefing@nature.com.

Thanks for reading,
Laura Woodrow, editor, Nature Briefing: Translational Research
With contributions by Flora Graham

summary.overview: 이번 이슈는 신약 효과 분석 방법, 관절염 치료 혁신, 정밀의학 개선 전략, 신속한 면역요법, 희귀 유전자 치료 승인 프레임워크 등 의학 분야의 주요 연구 동향을 다룹니다.
covered_item_titles: New way to analyse bodywide drug effects
A potential first for osteoarthritis treatment
Five pillars for bettering precision medicine
A new era for speedy immunotherapy
A blueprint for rare gene therapy approvals

article_briefings.title: New way to analyse bodywide drug effects
article_briefings.briefing_ko: 연구진이 동물 전반의 유전자 발현을 한 번에 측정할 수 있는 방법을 개발해, 약물이 다양한 기관에 미치는 영향을 분석하는 새로운 접근법을 제시했습니다.

article_briefings.title: A potential first for osteoarthritis treatment
article_briefings.briefing_ko: FDA에 제출된 신약이 첫 번째로 관절염 증상을 넘어 질병 자체를 치료하는 약으로 승인될 가능성이 있으며, 현재 약물은 주로 증상 완화에 중점을 둡니다.

article_briefings.title: Five pillars for bettering precision medicine
article_briefings.briefing_ko: 과학자들이 정밀의학의 실제 적용을 연구 성과와 맞추기 위해 질병 변이 이해, 일반화, 임상 시험 설계 등 5개 핵심 원칙을 제안했습니다.

article_briefings.title: A new era for speedy immunotherapy
article_briefings.briefing_ko: 스케일링이 용이한 새로운 CAR-T세포 치료법이 등장해, 기존 외부에서 제조하는 CAR-T세포보다 임상 적용 속도를 높일 수 있을 것으로 기대됩니다.

article_briefings.title: A blueprint for rare gene therapy approvals
article_briefings.briefing_ko: UNICORN 프레임워크가 희귀 유전자 치료의 과학적 잠재력과 실제 영향 사이의 격차 해소를 위한 승인 절차 개선 방안을 제시했습니다.

raw_metadata.subject: A new method to analyse bodywide drug effects
